data.title: Making Sense Of The Fed's Big Pause
data.description:
On Wednesday, May 7th, the Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) released its latest policy statement, holding the federal funds rate steady at 4.25% to 4.50%.

Alongside the usual boilerplate language, the Fed reiterated its intent to reduce securities holdings and its commitment to both sides of its dual mandate: supporting maximum employment and returning inflation to its 2 percent objective.

What stood out, however, was the Fed’s unusually direct acknowledgment of the rising economic uncertainty.

In its own words, the committee is now “attentive to the risks to both sides of its dual mandate” and believes that “the risks of higher unemployment and higher inflation have risen.” That marks one of the Fed’s clearest warnings yet about the potential onset of stagflation.

But as always, the official statement only tells part of the story. The real insights often come from the press conference. Here’s our take on some key excerpts from Chairman Jerome Powell’s remarks following the Fed’s two-day policy meeting.

The Fed Is Actively Choosing Patience

"We think our policy rate is in a good place… the appropriate thing to do is to wait and see how things evolve… we don’t think we need to be in a hurry."

Powell's stance was deliberately measured. He made it clear that the FOMC sees no pressing need to move rates in either direction right now. Even as risks evolve, the committee’s decision to hold is less about confidence in stability and more about the cost of acting prematurely.

"There’s so much uncertainty… we’re going to be watching the data… the data may move quickly or slowly, but we think we’re in a good position to let things evolve."

This is an admission that the path forward is murky. The Fed is leaning on flexibility, not a firm outlook. It's a tacit acknowledgement of the high uncertainty in the market right now, emphasizing a quick response over pre-emptive measures.

Why the Fed Won’t Move Preemptively This Time

“It’s not a situation where we can be preemptive because we actually don’t know what the right response to the data will be until we see more data.”

When asked whether the Fed might cut rates in anticipation of worsening conditions, Powell clearly pushed back. He contrasted today’s environment with 2019, when the Fed executed a series of precautionary rate cuts amid weakening growth and inflation well below target. That context allowed room to act early.

“2019 we did cut three times… you had a weakening economy and you had inflation at 1.6%. That’s a situation where you can move preemptively.”

Today, Powell noted, inflation remains above the 2% target. Forecasts from both the Fed and private sector point to renewed inflationary pressure, especially if tariffs are sustained. That puts policymakers in a bind.

“Now we have inflation running above target… and we have an expectation… that we’ll see upward pressure on inflation. Most forecasters are forecasting an increase in inflation.”

Until a clear direction emerges in the data, the Fed is unwilling to guess. The message is that preemptive easing is off the table for now. For the Fed, patience isn’t just a tactical decision. It’s the default stance when risks are two-sided and the path is still forming.

Stagflation Risk Is Explicitly on the Radar

"We may find ourselves in the challenging scenario in which our dual mandate goals are in tension… the risks of higher unemployment and higher inflation have risen."

This was Powell’s most direct acknowledgment that the economy could be entering a phase of conflicting pressures, where responding to one problem makes the other worse. That is the classic definition of stagflation risk.

"If the large increases in tariffs that have been announced are sustained, they’re likely to generate a rise in inflation, a slowdown in economic growth, and an increase in unemployment."

Rather than treating this as an abstract possibility, Powell linked the risk to concrete policy events already in motion. The Fed isn’t predicting stagflation, but it’s preparing for the conditions that could lead to it.

Sentiment Is Cracking, But the Data Hasn’t Followed - Yet

“People are feeling stress and concern… but unemployment hasn’t gone up, job creation is fine, wages are in good shape.”

Powell acknowledged the growing anxiety across households and businesses in response to rising tariffs and economic uncertainty. But he was clear: the Fed won’t respond to sentiment alone. What matters is whether those concerns translate into measurable economic deterioration.

“We see sentiment—there are concerns that higher prices may be coming… but that shock hasn’t hit yet.”

When pressed about what would trigger action, Powell returned to the same position: the Fed needs to see confirmation in hard data before making any move. Until then, the tone of the consumer or the headlines at the port aren’t enough to shift policy.

“We’re going to be looking at not just the sentiment data, but also the real economic data… it’s not at all clear what the appropriate response for monetary policy is at this time.”

The message is consistent: anxiety is rising, but the Fed is watching results, not reactions. Until labor markets or inflation prints force their hand, the policy stance remains firmly in wait-and-see mode.

Tariffs Are Now the Central Macro Variable

"The tariff increases announced so far have been significantly larger than anticipated… if sustained, they’re likely to generate a rise in inflation… a slowdown in economic growth… and an increase in unemployment."

Powell didn’t hedge here. He clearly connected trade policy not just as a risk factor, but as a potential driver of macro deterioration. This was more direct than the typical Fedspeak.

"There’s so much uncertainty about the scale, scope, timing, and persistence of the tariffs… it’s not at all clear what the appropriate response for monetary policy is at this time."

The Fed’s ability to respond is constrained not just by the data, but by a shifting fiscal landscape it doesn’t control. Powell repeatedly emphasized that tariff outcomes are shaping the Fed’s thinking, but without clarity, monetary policy will remain on pause, even as the risks accumulate.

Disclosure

This information is not an offer to sell, or solicitation of an offer to buy any investment product, security, or services offered by Jay Hatfield, or Infrastructure Capital Advisors, LLC, (“ICA”) or its affiliates. ICA, will only conduct such solicitation of an offer to buy any investment product or service offered by ICA, if at all, by (1) purported definitive documentation (which will include disclosures relating to investment objective, policies, risk factors, fees, tax implications and relevant qualifications), (2) to qualified participants, if applicable, and (3) only in those jurisdictions where permitted by law. This discussion is intended to provide insight into stocks and the market for entertainment and information purposes only, and is not a solicitation of any kind. data.title: U.S. Inflation Dips To Lowest Level Since 2021 As Tariffs Loom
data.description:
The US consumer price index, the market's preferred measure of inflation, is rising at the slowest rate in more than four years, but economists believe consumers will soon see the effects of President Donald Trump's tariffs.

The US consumer price index increased 2.3% year-over-year in April, the third month of annual declines in inflation growth and the smallest increase since February 2021, the Bureau of Labor Statistics reported May 13. Core consumer price inflation, which removes volatile energy and food prices, increased 2.8% year-over-year, nearly unchanged from the annual growth a month before.

The data shows a clear slowing in inflation as the rate of rising prices nears the Federal Reserve's 2% growth target, but the latest data will likely not prove to be a turning point for the domestic economy with tariffs looming, Lale Akoner, global market analyst with eToro, said in an interview. Cheaper oil and the largest decline in grocery prices since 2020 helped cool inflation in April, but housing costs remain high, services are climbing and any pricing reprieve last month was at least partly assisted by retailers clearing pre-tariff inventory, according to Akoner.

"That buffer may not last," Akoner said.

There were some modest increases in furniture and electronics prices in April, but outside of that, there was no widespread evidence that tariffs were impacting the inflation data yet, according to Thomas Simons, chief US economist at Jefferies.

"Consumers may have pulled forward demand, offering retailers some opportunity to reduce discounting, but frankly, the increases are small enough that they are hard to distinguish from month-to-month noise," Simons said in an interview.

China and the US on May 12 agreed to reduce their proposed tariffs on goods for an initial 90-day period, a trade truce that could prevent a steep spike in inflation later this year but will not prevent inflation from moving up from current levels, economists said.

Due to this truce, James Knightley, chief international economist with ING, said in an interview that he now sees year-over-year core inflation peaking at just below 4%, rather than at 4.5% to 4.7% that he had forecast before the 90-day pause. Knightley expects the consumer price index to end the year up 3.7% and eventually fall to the Fed's 2% target by the end of 2026.

Tariff wave

Under the Biden administration, the majority of imports entered the US with no or minimal duties, as the effective tariff rate was about 2.3%. The Trump administration has moved this rate to a universal 10% baseline and an overall effective or average tariff rate about five times the Biden administration's rate, Akoner with eToro said.

It remains unclear whether Trump's tariff regime will cause a temporary increase in prices or more persistent inflation, but Akoner believes the true impact of these tariffs likely will not show up in the data until June or July due to imports already in transit being priced pre-tariff, a one- to two-month lag in customs data and a two- to three-month delay for government inflation data to full reflect pass-through to prices, Akoner said.

"The tariff wave will likely hit in stages, with visible price effects starting around June or July, but expectations and market reactions are already underway," Akoner said. "If Trump's policies remain in force and expand, and especially if China retaliates, the third quarter of 2025 could see the first real inflation pressure tied to tariffs."

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Killam Apartment REIT: Bargains Galore In Apartment Landlords
data.description:
All values are in CAD unless noted otherwise.

Killam Apartment REIT (KMP.UN:CA) owns and operates a portfolio of apartment buildings. As per the most recent data, the multi-resident apartment portfolio accounts for 90.5% of the net operating income [NOI] for Killam. The balance is bought in by manufactured home communities [MHCs] (4.2%) and commercial properties (5.3%). This REIT has the distinction of having the largest residential presence in Atlantic Canada, with 174 of its 217 apartment properties located in the provinces of Nova Scotia, New Brunswick, Newfoundland and Labrador and Prince Edward Island. Ontario houses 27 properties, followed by 11 in Alberta and 5 in British Columbia. Over the last few years, Killam has been increasing its presence outside Atlantic Canada. The following graphic shows the progress made by this REIT since 2018 and where it plans to be positioned in 2028.

Killam pays out 6 cents in dividends on a monthly basis and based on the current price of $17.68, it yields 4.07%.

The current yield is in the same ballpark as Minto Apartment Real Estate Investment Trust (MI.UN:CA) which yields 4.03%, and a lot higher than Boardwalk Real Estate Investment Trust (BEI.UN:CA) with its sub 2.50% payout. We covered the Q1 results of both very recently (Boardwalk, Minto). While Minto has been battered and Boardwalk has left its peers in the dust over the last five years, Killam has returned a decent 33% to its investors.

Prior Coverage

We covered Killam around the end of last year. It was trading at a 25% discount to NAV (it's even cheaper now). We concluded that piece with our price and return targets for the next two years.

It seems that is a low-risk 7%-8% annual return setup from here. The dividend itself is at 4.2% and it would be fairly unusual for the funds from operations (FFO) and NAV to grow by less than 3% from here on out. You are likely to do better than that as the REIT will likely trade at NAV at some point down the line. We bought some and are now upgrading this to a BUY with a $20 price target in two years.

Source: Killam REIT: Quality Apartment Play At A Good Discount

It has had a decent performance since then and outperformed the broader markets as well.

The first quarter results were released last week. Today, we dive into them to see if the story is playing out as we expected.

Q1-2025

Powered by the lease-up of a couple of properties, Q1-2025 ended with an overall portfolio occupancy of 97.4% compared to 96.0% at March 31, 2024. Killam achieved an average rental increase of 5.1% on its apartment portfolio, which was slightly lower than the 5.4% bump that it earned last year.

Q4-2024 saw a weighted average rental rate increase of 7.9%. So while it remained lucrative in Q1, the mark-to-market rental spreads have been going down after peaking in 2024.

Some of the variance with Q4-2024 is also due to a high number of lease renewals on January 1. These renewals at the beginning of the year have more leases with rent controls on them, so Killam typically sees higher average rental rates in the subsequent calendar quarters. Coming back to the quarter that we are reviewing today, the revenue from the total rental portfolio increased by 6.3% on a year-over-year basis.

This was despite the increase in rental incentives, driven by the two lease ups in Ontario and Calgary. The operating expenses increased by 4.8%, but one of the two drivers of the increase, utility expense, is expected to be lower for the foreseeable future with the removal of the federal carbon tax effective April 1. The net operating income [NOI] increased on a year-over-year basis by 7.2%, and the funds from operations [FFO] by 9.1%.

The total FFO pot was shared by a higher number of units during the quarter, resulting in it trickling down to a 7.7% increase, on a per-unit basis. With 18 cents in distribution, Killam paid out 64% of its FFO. This would be of relative appeal to the income investors, as opposed to a REIT like Boardwalk that retains two thirds of its FFO.

Most of Killam's debt is from mortgages on the properties. At March 31, Killam's weighted average interest rate was 3.48%, a 3 basis points increase from December 31, 2024 and a 25 basis points increase from March 31, 2024.

The REIT refinanced a mortgage during the quarter at 3.67%. If we overshoot and apply a 4.50% rate for the balance mortgages maturing in 2025 and 2026, it will increase the interest expenses by around 13%.

We do this form of stress-testing to see how vulnerable any REIT is to higher rates. The amount here is higher than the 6% impact we saw for Minto. This is because Killam is starting at a lower current rate for 2025 than Minto and more importantly, only 5% of Minto's mortgages are up for renewal in the next two years. The CMHC rate at March 31 was 3.33%, so with over 83% of the Killam's mortgages insured, our 13% increase is way more drastic than the actual numbers will likely be. Running the above scenario with a 3.75% rate instead of 4.50%, gets us to an interest expense increase of around 6–7%. Coming back to Q1, Killam was solidly placed as far as the overall debt metrics were concerned, with minor improvements in most.

Overall, the REIT delivered a solid quarter.

Verdict

Currently, all Canadian apartment REITs are trading at a discount, some more than others. Discounts for a few of them can be seen below.

On some occasions, we dive deep into the NAV estimates, and this is especially true when we disagree with the NAV estimates the REIT uses. In the above cases, we agree more or less with the REITs' internal estimates. In other words, the discount is real. So which do you buy?

REITs like Boardwalk do not have a preponderance of rent control properties, unlike say a Canadian Apartment REIT (CAR.UN:CA). Killam has a balanced mix of the two. Investors that believe the market rents have peaked would prefer CAR.UN, as they benefit from a gradual increase to market rents, leaving a lot more juice in the tank from here on out. Those that believe the housing shortage will persist, will prefer REITs like Boardwalk to avoid delayed gratification. Killam can provide the best of both worlds. We own quite a few of these REITs, including Killam. We got in at an average price of $16 for this one and think it still provides a good bargain. We rate it a buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: FTAI Aviation Stock: Love Or Hate It - Still A Buy
data.description:
FTAI Aviation (NASDAQ:FTAI) stock has been a Wall Street darling for some time, but since invalid concerns were raised, the stock price has failed to snap back. I addressed those concerns in a prior report and the stock price has increased 15% from those levels, but the strong momentum in the stock price did not return.

In this report, I will discuss the most recent earnings to assess whether we still see a positive backdrop for FTAI Aviation or whether it is time to look for other investment opportunities.

FTAI Aviation's EBITDA Grows More Than 40%

During the first quarter, sales grew 54% year-on-year to $502 million. Aerospace Products sales rose 93% to $365 million as the company refurbished 138 modules. Lease income increased 28.6% to $68.5 million while maintenance revenues increased 8.3% to $49.6 million and revenues from asset sales declined 51% to $18.9 million.

EBITDA rose to 7% sequentially and 64% year-on-year to $269 million, which included $162 million from the leasing business and $130.9 million from the Aerospace Products business and a negative contribution of $17.4 million from corporate and intra-company eliminations. If we look a bit deeper into the results, the sequential EBITDA growth of 7% may look somewhat less impressive. EBITDA increased by $16.6 million and included a $30 million non-recurring settlement related to leasing assets in Russia. Excluding this item, higher corporate & other eliminations offset the growth that was seen for Aerospace Products. The increase in eliminations was in part driven because FTAI Aviation is transferring lease assets to its strategic partnership with ATLAS. That caused a $7 million intra-entity elimination.

At the same time, we note that vessel sales boosted corporate results by $18.7 million in Q4 2024. If we remove the non-recurring items, but leave in the eliminated gains on sales because it is likely going to be a recurring item as FTAI offloads assets to the strategic partnership, we see that sequential EBITDA growth was only 2.3%.

So, the strong aerospace product sales growth is offset by offloading the lease assets, which lowers lease revenues from the leasing segment. What I do like is that the business will more and more become a pure MRO play from an operating perspective, but my concern is increasingly becoming that the Aerospace Products growth is going to be offset by the strategic shift of moving away from its leasing segment.

FTAI Aviation Expects No Material Impact From Tariffs

Many aerospace companies initially saw their stock prices crumble under tariff fears for aerospace parts. That fear has largely disappeared, as aerospace parts should be exempt from tariffs through USMCA. The same holds for FTAI Aviation, enabling them to reiterate their $1.1-$1.15 billion EBITDA guidance for 2025 and $1.4 billion for 2026.

The company also has facilities in Montreal and Rome that could adopt a local-for-local set up if they aren’t already working in that way, and the facility in Montreal is expected to ramp up production in the balance of the year.

So, there is a lot of good stuff going on. At the same time, I am not too sure how to feel about the asset light lease model. I covered another company, Chorus Aviation, that tried to use this model, and it did not work out for them. Likely the FTAI Aviation set up comes in a somewhat different shape as it is a partnership and not necessarily an acting manager role in the lease set up, and FTAI Aviation is likely to cater to the engine requirements for the lease assets, but we really have to see how this partnership with Apollo will actually work out apart from providing FTAI Aviation with fewer assets on the balance sheet.

Is FTAI Stock Still A Buy?

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions, and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Wall Street analysts have a strong buy rating on FTAI Aviation with a $165.59 price target. I am a bit less optimistic on the price target and believe the stock price target should be $143.66, implying 21% upside, primarily driven by higher cash burn in 2024. I expect EBITDA to grow by 22.4% annually and free cash flow to improve significantly, providing support to higher share prices. So, our price target did come down from the $167 set initially, but upside remains.

We do note that the peer group valuation provides no upside for 2025, but does provide upside for the years after. At the same time, it is also important to note that the target for 2026 closely coincides with the valuation based on the company's median EV/EBITDA, and there is no company that is quite comparable to FTAI Aviation. The peer group, therefore, provides a valuation multiple that is in line with that of lessors, but FTAI Aviation is not a pure-play lessor.

Conclusion: FTAI Aviation Still Offers Value

I believe that FTAI Aviation still offers value. The shift to an asset light model is not something that I am completely charmed by, but it does mean that the company can offload lease assets and bolster its cash position. The company will keep its leasing segment, but the leasing business will primarily be scaled through the strategic partnership.

I believe that the company’s value remains understated and believe the stock remains a buy.

data.title: Apple: Facing Services Margin Erosion In The Name Of Justice (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Since my last analysis of Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL), the stock has gained 18% in price.

Apple's $3 trillion market valuation is significantly supported by its App Store business. This business is a cornerstone of its Services segment, which commands an approximately 30% commission on big-business transactions at this time, with a 15% fee applying to smaller companies. But now, recent court rulings and regulatory mandates threaten to dismantle this high-margin enterprise. In the U.S., a significant ruling against Apple in its long-standing legal battle with Epic Games mandates the immediate cessation of anti-competitive practices. Concurrently, the European Union's Digital Markets Act ("DMA") compels Apple to allow third-party app stores and sideloading (allowing third-party app marketplaces to install on Apple devices). Jurisdictions like Brazil and the UK are also initiating similar actions, indicating a broader trend of regulatory convergence against Apple's market practices. The major implication of these developments is that Apple's high-margin revenue streams are under threat, potentially having significant knock-on effects on the company's valuation. This comes amid an already largely sentiment-sustained valuation which is rooted in goodwill and brand power with somewhat deteriorating growth fundamentals. That said, the company still commands a rich moat and impressive ecosystem effects, which ought not to be ignored.

Regulatory Impact Analysis

Apple's App Store significantly contributes to its Services revenue. Services generated approximately $96.17 billion in FY24, up from $85.2 billion in FY23. The App Store alone probably generated about $10 billion in commissions in FY24 from U.S. operations alone. In total, about 1/3 of Apple's Services revenue is likely from the App Store. Given this significant contribution, recent regulatory actions pose a material risk to Apple's current operational model and bull-case growth outlook.

In the U.S., the April 2025 court ruling explicitly prohibits Apple from obstructing developers' ability to direct users to external payment systems. This ruling overturns Apple's efforts to levy a new 27% commission on transactions processed externally, marking a significant legal setback. If developers broadly adopt external payments, Apple could face substantial erosion of its multi-billion-dollar U.S.-based App Store revenue stream.

In Europe, due to the DMA, Apple is now mandated to allow third-party app marketplaces and direct app sideloading on its iOS ecosystem. This fundamentally undermines the exclusivity historically central to its monetization strategy. This leads to Apple's commission rate sometimes falling to ~17%, sometimes to 10%, or other times potentially eliminated if developers fully migrate to external distribution channels. As partial migration strategies, Apple has implemented new fees, like the €0.50 annual fee per app installation surpassing one million and a 3% payment processing fee for internal transactions. But even with these additional levies, significant revenue shortfalls are inevitable.

Similar regulatory frameworks are emerging in other global markets. Brazilian regulators have mandated comparable measures to those in Europe, with substantial daily fines for non-compliance. There's also growing evidence that the UK's Competition and Markets Authority is aligning with the EU's regulatory stance. In light of this international structural attack on Apple's profitability, I believe investors have a reason to be cautious on Apple, despite its robust market sentiment factors that keep its stock price relatively secure from major fundamentally induced declines.

In fiscal terms, a moderate global reduction to a 20% commission scenario implies an immediate revenue loss of approximately $9 billion annually. Given the App Store's notably high incremental margins (estimated between 70% and 80%), this translates to roughly $6–7 billion of gross profit erosion annually, equating to a significant EPS impact of about $0.40–0.50 on Apple's earnings per share. More aggressive scenarios (like commission reductions to 15% or even 10% globally) could further exacerbate revenue losses and margin compressions, potentially reducing EPS by as much as $0.60–1.00.

I believe the most obvious general counter to this structural challenge would be to successfully integrate strong innovation within Apple's culture. Though, this is easier said than done. As it stands, Apple's walled garden is certainly under siege, and investors are holding on to a stock that is vulnerable to decline in theory, even if not in practice. I'd feel more secure holding a stock that is both theoretically/fundamentally secure and practically so.

Valuation Analysis

For the purposes of this analysis, I will consider the Services-based regulatory impact as the highest contributing factor of near-term P/E compression. Other P/E compression is perhaps most notable from the U.S. manufacturing revitalization and anti-China trade policies. However, Apple will likely be circumnavigating these depletive effects by migrating much of its supply chain to India, Vietnam, and even somewhat to the States.

In scenario one, I consider a 20% commission, which appears the most likely sustained current outcome and is my base-case supposition. This suggests revenue loss of approximately $9 billion annually, reducing net income by roughly 7%, translating to the aforementioned $0.40–0.50 GAAP EPS impact. Still, GAAP EPS will likely come in above $7.50 for FY26. Apple's valuation might contract from the present ~30 TTM figure to ~27.5. This leads to a base-case price target of ~$215, if we use a $7.65 GAAP EPS estimate and a P/E ratio of 28.

In scenario two, I consider a 15% commission, which would be the result of further mounting and compounding pressure from companies and regulatory bodies taking aim at Apple's supposed anti-competitive behavior. Revenue declines of about $13 billion annually appears likely here, with net income down by around 10%, and GAAP EPS dropping by about $0.65. Still, FY26 GAAP EPS will likely be above $7.25. As this would substantially impact the company's earnings trajectory, a further P/E contraction toward 25x or below is not unlikely.

In scenario three, I take an extreme view for the purposes of downside-risk awareness. Apple's App Store revenue could be slashed by about $18 billion on an annual basis. This would equate to about a 15–18% net income erosion and profound EPS declines by ~$1. FY26 GAAP EPS could come in below $7. The downside potential here could be stark, with severe P/E compression that the company would need to offset through AI-related internal efficiency gains, like operational leanness, and also the aforementioned revitalization of innovation. This negative outcome is low-risk, at less than a 10% possibility.

Conclusion: Hold

Apple's App Store business model is definitely facing severe regulatory siege. This requires a reevaluation of both its financial prospects and market valuation. This is undeniably true, given that anti-competitive practices in the U.S. and Europe represent not merely a temporary setback but potentially a structural reshaping of Apple's high-margin Services segment. Apple could circumvent these issues with strategic compliance wins, innovation in revenue models, or even broader strategic pivots. Such approaches will largely determine the resilience of Apple's valuation. My base-case target outlines a $215 12-month stock price, and this is very likely. That said, the downside risks are too severe and the company's innovation level is lower than I would like for a Buy rating. As such, I offer a much more conservative Hold rating. Albeit, this is an upgrade from my prior Sell rating, as I have started to acknowledge the stickiness of Apple stock's sentiment amid its strong product ecosystem, brand, and shareholder-friendly stock buybacks.

Net Expenses Excluding Investment Related Expenses for Institutional shares: 0.86%; for Investor A shares: 1.11%.

Commentary as of 03/31/25

  • The fund posted returns of 1.31% (Institutional shares) and 1.25% (Investor A shares, without sales charge) for the first quarter of 2025.
  • Stock selection in China and Taiwan detracted from relative performance, while stock picking in South Africa and an overweight exposure to Hungary contributed. Security selection in the consumer discretionary and information technology (IT) sectors hampered relative returns the most, and stock selection in the materials sector was helpful.
  • By country, the largest overweight position was in Mexico, followed by Indonesia and Hungary, while the largest underweight exposures were to India, Saudi Arabia, Taiwan. At the sector level, the fund had overweight positions in IT and real estate, and underweight holdings in energy and communication services.

Contributors

Detractors

South African miner Gold Fields was the largest contributor. Gold prices continued to rally as investors flocked to perceived safe-haven investments due to concerns about tariffs and a pick-up in volatility. We took profits on the position during the quarter. An overweight exposure to Hungary’s OTP Bank was also beneficial. The stock performed well while it was expected to benefit from any resolution of the Russia-Ukraine conflict. Pan-Asian insurer Prudential was buoyed by news of a possible listing of its ICICI Prudential Asset Management joint venture in India.

 Puma was the top detractor from relative performance. The company fell short of preliminary earnings expectations and reported a decline in net profit for 2024. An underweight allocation to Chinese internet group Tencent also hurt relative returns. The stock benefited from the improved prospects of its cloud services business, given potential artificial intelligence ((AI)) benefits, and the strong performance of its gaming business. We reduced the position in January after the company was added to the U.S. Department of Defense’s list of companies with suspected ties to China’s military. Elsewhere, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing detracted due to concerns about a demand slowdown and tariff risks.

Further insight

During the quarter, we reduced the fund’s underweight exposure to Chinese e-commerce group Alibaba, as we saw more growth drivers linked to AI large language models and cloud computing. We were also positive about another share buyback. We added some resilience to the fund through Silvercorp Metals, as we believed that increased geopolitical tensions, coupled with tariff risks, would support metals pricing. We exited the position in Prudential to reduce some exposure to China ahead of the U.S. administration’s new tariff announcements on April 2.

data.title: Amazon Is Quietly Building An AI Empire
data.description:
In the year 2000, when Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) was still struggling to become profitable and many analysts (amazon.bomb) were writing it off as "an online bookstore with too many costs," Jeff Bezos quipped, "We don't think short term. We plant seeds for a forest." 25 years later, the forest is still alive and kicking. It's a great example of how long-term thinking trumps short-term thinking. Amazon now sells almost everything from A to Z. It's also become a major player in infrastructure, from cloud computing and AI to advertising and e-health.

Yet investors seem to have become more critical this year. AWS's growth is slowing, there are concerns about tariffs, and AI growth seems to be largely confined to Microsoft, Google, and OpenAI. Is Amazon too late for AI growth? Or are they quietly building something bigger? In this article, we explore whether Amazon is now ready to harvest after years of extreme expansion and investment, or whether caution is still warranted.

Amazon's Competition Is Getting Fiercer

Amazon remains by far the largest e-commerce player in the U.S. With a market share of over 40%, it is 6x and 19x larger in online sales than Walmart or Target, respectively. Amazon has strong economies of scale in logistics, a large assortment, and Prime that is hard to match. Prime has more than 200 million members worldwide who not only shop but also stream and use Alexa. Amazon uses smart suggestions that keep users on the platform. Every order, every click, every recommendation makes Amazon smarter.

Still, the competition is getting fiercer as Walmart combines a strong physical network with greater visibility, and Target innovates in "same-day pickup" with an improved customer experience. And on top of that, (Chinese) platforms like Shein and Temu are attacking Amazon's price competition head-on. In early 2025, the tide seemed to turn in Amazon's favor when the U.S. ended the so-called "de minimis" regulation, which suddenly taxed cheap imports under $800 with hefty import duties of up to 145%. This hit Chinese e-commerce companies hard. However, that advantage has temporarily weakened: as of May 14, the US and China have agreed to temporarily reduce these tariffs. For 90 days, the tariffs will be reduced from 145% to 30%, allowing platforms like Temu to make their prices more competitive again. But for now, it is uncertain whether this reduction will be permanent because after this period the agreements will be re-evaluated.

AWS: Slowing Growth, Broad Strategy

Amazon Web Services (AWS) remains Amazon's financial growth powerhouse. In the first quarter of 2025, AWS posted revenue growth of 17% to $29.3 billion, with a margin of 39%, good for a strong operating profit of $11.5 billion. However, rival Microsoft's Azure grew faster (revenue growth of +33%), which put pressure on Amazon's dominant position, but AWS remains the broadest cloud solution and strong in Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaS).

In addition, AWS is key to Amazon's AI strategy. AI requires enormous computing power, and that computing power is purchased from AWS. With Trainium (Amazon's own AI chip), Bedrock (generative AI platform) and a portfolio of basic models (including Claude, LLaMa and Amazon's Nova models), Amazon is strongly committed to AI as a growth catalyst.

Andy Jassy now calls AI a "triple-digit growth, billion-dollar business." Amazon's strategy is broad: it builds models (Nova), sells computing power (Trainium), provides infrastructure (Bedrock), and applies AI to Alexa, distribution, advertising, and even quantum computing (the Ocelot chip). This sets Amazon apart from competitors, who often focus on just one layer of AI.

Advertising Remains A Strong Revenue Model

Amazon's advertising segment grew 19% to $13.9 billion in the first quarter. Advertisers buy access to an audience of hundreds of millions of Prime members. Click-through rates in certain categories are high, targeting is precise, and margins are impressive. Analysts expect Amazon's ad revenue to surpass YouTube's within a few years.

Other Growth Catalysts: Kuiper, Alexa, Health

Amazon has other growth catalysts in store. Project Kuiper, Amazon's own Starlink competitor, has launched its first satellites and expects to be operational this year. Prime Video is expanding into sports, movies (including a new James Bond franchise) and advertising. Its healthcare business (One Medical) offers economies of scale in a fragmented market. In short, Amazon is building growth outside of retail. This allows Amazon to reduce its business risks and enjoy higher margins in other areas of its business.

Strong Results But Moderate Outlook

The first quarter of 2025 was strong, with revenue growing 9% YoY to $155.7B and net income of $17.1B (+64% YoY). All segments contributed to this growth. AWS grew solidly and the advertising market even faster. Retail showed an increase in operating margin, both in North America and internationally. Notably, even the international arm (which had been loss-making for a long time) posted a profit of $1B.

Capital expenditures were $24.3 billion in the first quarter, largely driven by investments in technology and distribution. Amazon expects capital expenditures to increase in 2025, particularly in AI infrastructure and data centers. This weighs on free cash flow, which fell to $25.9 billion on a TTM basis. However, operating cash flow is robust ($113 billion TTM) and Amazon has a strong balance sheet with $94 billion in cash.

The outlook for the second quarter of 2025 is cautious. Amazon expects revenue growth of around 5% to 8%, with operating profit expected to be $13-17.5 billion. The pressure on the outlook is partly due to one-time costs (Kuiper, stock compensation) and not by weakness in operating activities.

Attractive Valuation

Amazon is currently very attractively valued, with a forward P/E ratio of 34x expected 2025 earnings. That may not seem like a bargain but it is attractive from a historical perspective. Its EV/EBITDA of 18x is below the 5-year average of 26x and even below Walmart's (EV/EBITDA = 20x), despite Amazon's higher margins and growth prospects. This makes Amazon appear undervalued relative to its peers, while its growth potential is many times greater.

Why Caution Is Advised

While Amazon has a strong long-term outlook, there are still some concerns. AWS's growth is slowing and competitors like Microsoft are gaining ground, putting pressure on Amazon's dominant position as a market leader.

Meanwhile, Amazon requires significant investments. The large capital injections into infrastructure and AI make sense given its ambitions but are putting a heavy burden on free cash flow. Analysts don't expect a recovery until 2026, meaning current returns on investment are limited for those expecting quick results.

Risks outside Amazon's control also play a role. Recent tariffs on Chinese goods could erode margins, as could a slowing US economy. While Amazon has been flexible in responding to changes, consumer sentiment is cautious.

Conclusion

Amazon is in a transition from rapid growth to capital efficiency. Core segments are growing profitably and new initiatives offer additional growth. The stock is not cheap compared to the market as a whole but is fairly valued given its quality, scale and cash flow potential.

For investors with a 3-5 year horizon, Amazon offers a unique combination of AI, cloud, retail and advertising. The broad diversification offers safety in times of economic stagnation and Amazon offers enough value in these segments to guarantee a safe investment. Those who are willing to hold the stock for a few years are buying a dominant player with a broad and strong growth path.

For certain share classes, BlackRock may voluntarily agree to waive certain fees and expenses in which the adviser may discontinue at any time without notice. Expenses stated as of the fund’s most recent prospectus. Data represents past performance and is no guarantee of future results. Investment returns and principal values may fluctuate so that an investor s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. All returns assume reinvestment of dividends and capital gains. Current performance may be lower or higher than that shown. Refer to Investment Management & Financial Services | BlackRock for most recent month-end performance. Investment returns reflect total fund operating expenses, net of all fees, waivers and/or expense reimbursements. Index performance is shown for illustrative purposes only. It is not possible to invest directly in an unmanaged index. Share classes have different sales charges, fees and other features. Returns with sales charge reflect deduction of current maximum initial sales charge of 4.25% for Investor A shares. Institutional shares have no front- or back-end load. Institutional shares have limited availability and may be purchased at various minimums. See prospectus for details. Net Expenses Excluding Investment Related Expenses for Institutional shares: 0.43%; for Investor A shares: 0.68%.

Commentary as of 03/31/25

  • The fund posted returns of -0.20% (Institutional shares) and -0.26% (Investor A shares, without sales charge) for the first quarter of 2025.
  • Income accrual canceled out negative price action as yields rose across the curve. The fund held a shorter duration position (lower sensitivity to interest rate movements) for most of the quarter. Negatively convex structures (where duration lengthens as rates rise) performed positively as rates rose. Higher current yield accrual benefits the fund when yield contributes a greater portion of the total return than price appreciation, with the quarter characterized by depreciation.
  • During the quarter, the fund had a quality bias and the cash and cash equivalent reserve declined to 4.4% from 7.0%. Duration of 6.85 years was a quarter of a year above the benchmark, while 16.6% of securities were exposed to alternative minimum tax. Leverage (borrowed capital) from tender option bonds was stable at 0.9%. The fund had overweight allocations to the corporate-backed municipal and transportation sectors.

Contributors

Detractors

The fund’s high income accrual, negatively convex exposures, and higher-quality bias were positive contributors. Longer maturity securities performed poorly as longer-term rates increased and the yield curve steepened. A robust new issue calendar allowed the managers to purchase attractively structured coupons at higher yields. The fund has become less negatively convex, which should provide better price appreciation potential in future rate declines. This was manifest in its higher duration posture than in past quarters.

Longer maturity, higher duration securities decreased in value as longer-term rates rose and the yield curve steepened. The market has become more concerned about longer maturities as the U.S. Treasury will debate how to finance larger deficits and roll over lower-yielding debt. High yield securities were a negative contributor as spreads for illiquid holdings widened, notably in the workforce housing, proton therapy, and charter school sectors. A 2.5% rates hedge also hampered relative performance.

Further insight

During the quarter, long-term interest rates rose, concerns about mutual fund cash flows increased, new issuance was robust, and questions were raised about the future tax treatment of the municipal asset class. Credit quality, while generally strong, is being debated at a local level as federal grants seem to be less certain with a new administration, and demographic trends work against the revenue portion of state and local budgets.

Consequently, municipal bonds are relatively inexpensive at this point, especially for longer maturities. This yield relationship should be monitored as the curve adjusts to future debt issuance forecasts.

★★★★ Morningstar OverallTM

data.title: Ignore The Noise - Novo Nordisk Is A Clear Buy
data.description:
In my last article about Novo Nordisk (NVO) I wrote, that the stock has probably declined enough. I already rated the stock as a "Buy" but the stock continued to decline, and it is now trading about 23% lower compared to February 2025 when my last article was published. In the conclusion of my last article, I wrote:

Summing up, I think we can be slightly optimistic again, and Novo Nordisk might be a "Buy" again. But it is a very cautious "Buy" and I still think that it is possible that the correction might continue, as Novo Nordisk is not cheap and certainly not a bargain at this point. But the current stock price can be justified, and growth could be high enough in the upcoming years to make this a good investment.

In the following article, we are looking at Novo Nordisk once again and answer the question if the decline was justified or if the steep decline—more than 55% from its previous all-time high—generated a buying opportunity for the stock.

Quarterly Results

Despite the 55% decline for Novo Nordisk (which, by the way, is the steepest decline since the 1980s), the company is still reporting great results and growing at a high pace. On May 7, 2025, Novo Nordisk reported first quarter results for fiscal 2025 and net sales increased from DKK 65,349 million in Q1/24 to DKK 78,087 in Q1/25—resulting in 19.5% year-over-year growth. Operating profit increased even 21.8% year-over-year from DKK 31,846 million in the same quarter last year to DKK 38,791 million in this quarter. And diluted earnings per share increased from DKK 5.68 in Q1/24 to DKK 6.53 in Q1/25—resulting in 15.0% year-over-year bottom-line growth.

Finally, free cash flow increased 89.1% year-over-year from DKK 5,020 million in Q1/24 to DKK 9,492 million in Q1/25. However, both metrics are rather low and must be seen as outliers.

Let's also look at the results in more detail, and we start by looking at the total insulin sales. In the recent past, insulin was not a driver of growth but in this quarter, sales increased 4.4% year-over-year to DKK 14,997 million in Q1/25. One of the drivers of growth were GLP-1 sales, which increased 13.1% year-over-year to DKK 39,574 million. Growth here was especially driven by Ozempic, which grew 17.7% year-over-year to DKK 32,721 million.

The highest growth rates stemmed from total obesity care, which increased revenue by 67.0% year-over-year to DKK 18,424 million. And while Saxenda sales declined steeply, the growth was especially driven by Wegovy. Sales of Wegovy grew by 85% year-over-year to DKK 17,360 million. And finally, rare disease sales increased 5% year-over-year to DKK 4,619 million.

Growth Slowing Down?

When looking at revenue growth in the last few years, we see that quarterly revenue growth is slowing down again. A few quarters ago, revenue growth was as high as 44%, and now it slowed down to slightly above 15% again (but let's be clear, these are still extremely high-growth rates and even for Novo Nordisk above-average growth rates).

And not only are growth rates slowing down, but management also cut its guidance—something that did not happen a lot in the recent past. Instead of 16% to 24% sales growth at constant exchange rates, Novo Nordisk is now expecting sales to grow between 13% to 21%. And reported sales growth will be 3% lower. The updated sales growth is especially reflecting lower-than-planned penetration of branded GLP-1 treatments in the United States. Additionally, the free cash flow is now expected to be only between DKK 56 billion and DKK 66 billion. In a previous guidance, the company expected free cash flow to be between DKK 75 billion and DKK 85 billion.

Although it seems like Novo Nordisk is facing problems (and the steeply declining stock price might also support that view), I think we should look at the company and stock from a different point of view. The stock was simply overvalued, and we saw a necessary correction, which brought the stock down almost to bargain territory again. We will look at the stock price (and valuation) later in this article—more important is the fundamental business and the question if Novo Nordisk really is in trouble.

For starters, analysts are still very optimistic about the next few years. We have to point out that analysts are not really getting more optimistic and expect more or less the same earnings per share for the next few years as a few quarters earlier. Nevertheless, analysts are expecting earnings per share to grow at a CAGR of 17.77% in the next five years until fiscal 2029.

Even when analysts are not getting more optimistic and management did cut guidance, a growth rate of 17% annually for the next five years is still an extremely high-growth rate. And when talking about growth we usually focus on diabetes, GLP-1 and obesity because these products drove revenue growth in the last few years for Novo Nordisk.

However, we should not ignore that Novo Nordisk is trying to expand its pipeline and product portfolio and might start selling pharmaceuticals for other therapeutic areas as well. When looking at Novo Nordisk's pipeline, we see an impressive pipeline with significant growth opportunities and a total of 44 candidates in phase 1, 2 or 3. Considering that Novo Nordisk is a business with about 10 major pharmaceuticals driving growth and being responsible for almost the entire revenue, having almost 50 pipeline candidates is very promising.

When looking at the pipeline, we see diabetes products (which are still responsible for the biggest part of revenue) are only a small part of the pipeline. A bigger part of the pipeline is focused on obesity care, although seven products are still in phase 1.

And I already mentioned this in previous articles, but Novo Nordisk is focusing more and more on cardiovascular and other emerging therapy areas and after shifting into obesity care a few years ago, we might see another "transformation" of the business.

For example, Novo Nordisk is trying to get semaglutide (which is used for Ozempic and Wegovy) to also be approved for the treatment of mesh. During the last earnings call, Martin Holst Lange—the head of development—made the following statement:

Within cardiovascular and emerging therapeutic areas, we are excited to have submitted the ESSENCE Part 1 trial with once-weekly semaglutide 2.4 milligram for regulatory approval in both EU and U.S. for the treatment of mesh and moderate to advanced liver fibrosis, namely F2 and F3.

As a reminder, it achieved both primary endpoints. By week 72, 37% of people treated with semaglutide 2.4 milligram achieved improved liver fibrosis with no worsening of the hepatitis while 63% achieved a resolution of CO2 hepatitis with no worsening of liver fibrosis. To put this into perspective, the ESSENCE Phase 3 trials, trial results are the best Phase 3 results within MESH to date.

With part 1 of the ESSENCE trial being completed and the submission, Novo Nordisk is expecting a decision by the FDA for the United States in the second half of 2025 (as FDA priority review was granted in the United States).

The next steps are the part 2 of the trial, which will be completed in 2029. However, this is only one example of the company's cardiovascular pipeline. And although it is very difficult to make predictions for the years to come, we can be quite confident that the rich pipeline Novo Nordisk has, will lead to growth and even if not all the pharmaceuticals are a success, it will lead to additional revenue, and some might be a success.

Novo Nordisk is also focusing on partnerships and strategic acquisitions. In my opinion, this is a major shift for Novo Nordisk as the business was mostly growing organically in previous decades. It is often a desperate act when companies are focusing on acquisitions. In many cases, the companies doing major acquisitions are in a phase where they can't grow organically anymore and are buying other companies for a lot of cash. The situation for Novo Nordisk is different: The company is still able to grow organically and growing at a very high pace, but it can make sense for Novo Nordisk to use the generated cash to make strategic acquisitions to continue growing at a high pace.

This strategic shift also had an impact on the balance sheet. A few years ago, we usually ignored Novo Nordisk's balance sheet as we knew the company had almost no debt and a great balance sheet. Now the situation is a little different and on March 31, 2025, Novo Nordisk had DKK 22,413 million in short-term borrowings as well as DKK 96,310 million in long-term borrowings.

But after subtracting DKK 38,938 million in "cash at bank" about DKK 80 billion in debt remains. And Novo Nordisk is generating almost enough free cash flow annually to repay the debt and with the business growing at a high pace, there is no reason to be worried about the balance sheet and company's debt.

Of course, we should not only look at new products and the pipeline, which might contribute to growth. We also have to look at the current products Novo Nordisk has and how revenue might decline after the loss of patent protection. However, Novo Nordisk is in a good position right now. Wegovy, Ozempic and Rybelsus are patent protected until 2031 in Europe and until 2032 in the United States. Additionally, Fiasp is patent protected until 2030. Although we don't have precise numbers for Fiasp right now, these four patent-protected products are responsible for more than 75% of revenue. Therefore, we can state that the current product portfolio and current revenue is well protected for the next few years.

Intrinsic Value Calculation

Aside from looking at the company and growth rates, we also must look at the stock price. When high-growth expectations are already baked in into the stock price, a company (and its stock) is not a good investment. In the case of Novo Nordisk, we use a discount cash flow calculation again. As always, we are calculating with a 10% discount rate, as this is the annual growth we like to achieve at least.

As basis for our calculation, we can use the free cash flow according to the guidance and take the midpoint of the guidance, which is DKK 61 billion. Additionally, we are calculating with 4,446 million in diluted shares outstanding. Novo Nordisk has to grow 14% annually for the next ten years followed by 4% growth until perpetuity to be fairly valued right now.

However, we must make some modification to this calculation. First, I see the free cash flow a little too pessimistic as basis and would rather calculate with DKK 66 billion - this is the upper end of the guidance for fiscal 2025. And in 2023, free cash flow was DKK 68,326 million and in 2024 it was DKK 67,456 million (which refers to free cash flow before acquisitions). This already leads to an intrinsic value of DKK 482 for the stock.

A second modification might be the growth rate until perpetuity. While I don't think we should calculate with a higher growth rate than 14% in the years to come (this is certainly not a low growth rate and one that might seem achievable for Novo Nordisk—in the last 10 years, it grew 16.22% annually). As I argued in my last article, we can use a higher growth rate for Novo Nordisk until perpetuity:

First, I know that we should not use a higher growth rate than 4% for perpetuity, but if there is any company where we can make the case for higher growth rates in 10, 20, or 30 years from now it is Novo Nordisk. Being 100 years old, Novo Nordisk is certainly a very mature business, but is proving again, and again, it can grow at a much higher pace than GDP or industry peers. Therefore, we can argue that Novo Nordisk can probably also grow at a higher pace than 4% in the upcoming decades (of course, that is all very speculative).

I often calculate with 6% growth until perpetuity in such cases, and that number is based on two different metrics. These charts are showing a long-term performance of the U.S. stock market around 7% (exemplified by the S&P 500). Sometimes growth rates were lower, sometimes they were a little higher. But even more important than the stock price performance is earnings per share growth. And when looking at the last century, earnings per share also grew with a CAGR of 6% over the long run (the number also depends on what timeframe we are looking at).

When calculating with 6% growth until perpetuity, we get an intrinsic value of DKK 653 and Novo Nordisk is clearly undervalued. At the time of writing, Novo Nordisk is trading about 35% below its intrinsic value.

Conclusion

In my last article, I already wrote that Novo Nordisk might be a cautious "Buy". In this article—and after the stock continued declining more than 20% compared to the previous article—I see Novo Nordisk clearly as a "Buy" and one of the better investments we can make right now. I won't rule out an even lower stock price for Novo Nordisk (and in one scenario I see the stock dropping even to DKK 300), but I think we can start buying now, and I probably will increase my position in the coming weeks again.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

Expenses for Institutional shares: Total 0.56%; Net, Including Investment Related Expenses (dividend expense, interest expense, acquired fund fees and expenses and certain other fund expenses) 0.44%. For Investor A shares: Total 0.80%; Net, Including Investment Related Expenses 0.69%. Institutional and Investor A shares have contractual waivers with an end date of 06/30/2026 terminable upon 90 days' notice. For certain share classes, BlackRock may voluntarily agree to waive certain fees and expenses in which the adviser may discontinue at any time without notice. Expenses stated as of the fund’s most recent prospectus. Data represents past performance and is no guarantee of future results. Investment returns and principal values may fluctuate so that an investor s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. All returns assume reinvestment of dividends and capital gains. Current performance may be lower or higher than that shown. Refer to blackrock.com for most recent month-end performance. Investment returns reflect total fund operating expenses, net of all fees, waivers and/or expense reimbursements. Index performance is shown for illustrative purposes only. It is not possible to invest directly in an unmanaged index. Share classes have different sales charges, fees and other features. Returns with sales charge reflect deduction of current maximum initial sales charge of 4% for Investor A shares. Institutional shares have no front- or back-end load. Institutional shares have limited availability and may be purchased at various minimums. See prospectus for details. Net Expenses Excluding Investment Related Expenses for Institutional shares: 0.43%; for Investor A shares: 0.68%.

Commentary as of 03/31/25

  • The fund posted returns of 2.75% (Institutional shares) and 2.56% (Investor A shares, without sales charge) for the first quarter of 2025.
  • Structured products and emerging market debt contributed to performance. U.S. rates, U.S. investment grade credit, and U.S. agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS) detracted.
  • We reduced top-line duration (sensitivity to interest rate movements) from an overweight position to a modest underweight. We preferred the front-end of the yield curve and held a steepening bias. The fund’s U.S. investment grade credit exposure was reduced and the agency MBS position was increased to overweight. In addition, we favored high- quality securitized exposure.

Contributors

Detractors

The allocation to securitized assets, specifically commercial mortgage-backed securities, was the largest contributor, as high-quality securitized spreads largely tightened for most of January and February. Emerging market positioning, particularly Mexican and Brazilian local rates, was beneficial as tariff concerns declined in January before reaccelerating toward quarter-end.

The U.S. rates position detracted from performance. As rates rallied in late January and into February, we held a steepening bias and reduced the overweight exposure, given our view that parts of the yield curve were overbought. Throughout February, the yield curve flattened and rates rallied, which caused the fund’s underweight position in the “belly” of the curve to detract. Positions in U.S. investment grade credit and agency MBS weighed on returns due to policy uncertainty and heightened rate volatility.

Further insight

During the quarter, the fund’s overweight duration exposure was reduced to a modest underweight position (5.84 years versus the benchmark’s 5.85 years). This was done largely by shifting from an overweight position in the 7-10-year part of the yield curve to an underweight exposure. We held a steepening bias in the belief that the long-end of the curve might sell off due to pressure on term premium expansion as a result of fiscal deficit concerns. We continued to prefer the front-end of the curve as yields appeared attractive. In spread sectors, we tactically rotated across sectors as we continued to look for attractive, high-quality sources of income.

Top 10 holdings (%)

Federal National Mortgage 12.48

Goldman Sachs Group Inc/The (GS) 0.83

Investment approach

Invests at least 80% of its assets in bonds and maintains an average portfolio duration that is within ±20% of the duration of the benchmark. The fund may invest up to 25% of its assets in foreign issuers, of which 10% may be invested in emerging markets issuers. Up to 10% of the fund’s assets may be exposed to non-U.S. currency risk.

Important Risks: The fund is actively managed and its characteristics will vary. Holdings shown should not be deemed as a recommendation to buy or sell securities. Bond values fluctuate in price so the value of your investment can go down depending on market conditions. Fixed income risks include interest-rate and credit risk. Typically, when interest rates rise, there is a corresponding decline in bond values. Credit risk refers to the possibility that the bond issuer will not be able to make principal and interest payments. Principal of mortgage- or asset- backed securities normally may be prepaid at any time, reducing the yield and market value of those securities. Obligations of U.S. gov't agencies are supported by varying degrees of credit but generally are not backed by the full faith and credit of the U.S. gov't. The fund may use derivatives to hedge its investments or to seek to enhance returns. Derivatives entail risks relating to liquidity, leverage and credit that may reduce returns and increase volatility. International investing involves special risks including, but not limited to political risks, currency fluctuations, illiquidity and volatility. These risks may be heightened for investments in emerging markets.

The opinions expressed are those of the fund’s portfolio management team as of March 31, 2025, and may change as subsequent conditions vary. Information and opinions are derived from proprietary and nonproprietary sources deemed by BlackRock to be reliable, are not necessarily all-inclusive and are not guaranteed as to accuracy.

BlackRock provides compensation in connection with obtaining or using third-party ratings and rankings.

1 Class R shares are sold to a limited group of investors, including certain retirement plans. See prospectus for details. 2 The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index comprises the total U.S. investment-grade bond market.

You should consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of the fund carefully before investing. The prospectus and, if available, the summary prospectus contain this and other information about the fund and are available, along with information on other BlackRock funds, by calling 800-882-0052 or from your financial professional. The prospectus should be read carefully before investing.
